# Introduction

Here is a quick summary of what this book covers.

In @sec-ch03-tokens, we tokenize text — converting raw characters into integer IDs using Byte Pair Encoding.
Without this step, the model would have no input to work with.

In @sec-ch04-embeddings, we build the embedding matrix, converting token IDs into dense vectors.
These learned vectors are the model's first representation of meaning.

In @sec-ch05-positional-encoding, we inject position information using sinusoidal positional encoding.
Without it, the model cannot tell the difference between "dog bites man" and "man bites dog."

In @sec-ch06-attention, we introduce attention — the engine of the transformer.
Each token learns to look at all other tokens and decide which ones are relevant.

In @sec-ch07-multi-head-attention, we run multiple attention heads in parallel — each free to specialize on a different kind of relationship in the text.

In @sec-ch08-feed-forward, we add the feed-forward network: a two-layer MLP applied identically to every token position.
This is where most of the model's factual knowledge is stored.

In @sec-ch09-transformer-block, we assemble the complete transformer block from attention, the feed-forward network, residual connections, and layer normalization — and stack `N` of them.

In @sec-ch10-vocab-projection, we complete the forward pass by projecting the final hidden state back into a probability distribution over the vocabulary.

In @sec-ch11-loss, we measure the model's predictions using cross-entropy loss, the single number that drives all of learning.

In @sec-ch12-training, we train the model — computing gradients via backpropagation and updating every weight with gradient descent.

*Appendix A* assembles the complete microGPT Python implementation in one runnable file.

*Appendix B* is a math primer covering every prerequisite — vectors, matrices, softmax, logarithms, and more.

Let's start at the very beginning.
